feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Academic portfolio of Wazih Yeaser Tanzim — wireless communications and machine learning for the physical layer (cell-free massive MIMO, beamforming, 6G and beyond).

entry.title: Again and Again and Again…..
entry.content:
The title of this post is a joke I stole from my own to-do list. For years the same three or four items just kept reappearing. Learn this. Finish that. Focus. I used to read the repetition as failure. Three books — James Clear’s Atomic Habits, Charles Duhigg’s The Power of Habit, and Cal Newport’s Deep Work — slowly convinced me it was the opposite. The repetition wasn’t the problem. It was the entire mechanism.

Start with the machinery. Duhigg’s central idea is that a habit is a loop with three parts: a cue that triggers it, a routine you run, and a reward that makes the loop worth repeating. Do it often enough and the brain stops deliberating — it hands the whole sequence to autopilot to save energy. Clear keeps the loop but sharpens it, slipping a fourth piece into the middle: craving. The cue doesn’t just start the routine; it starts a wanting, and the wanting is the engine. It is the same diagram drawn at higher resolution, and the extra piece matters, because craving is where a habit is actually won or lost.

What neither of them lets you keep is the flattering story we tell about success — that it comes from motivation, or willpower, or the occasional heroic burst of effort. Both books are quietly ruthless about this. Willpower runs out. Motivation is a mood. What’s left, when those fail, is whatever your habits do on their own. Clear’s version is that tiny actions, one per cent better, compound into results that look sudden only because nobody was watching the arithmetic. Duhigg’s is the “keystone habit” — the single routine that, once changed, quietly rewrites a dozen others. Different metaphors, same claim: success is mostly the residue of what you repeat without thinking.

But repetition alone isn’t the whole story, and this is where the third book earns its place. Newport’s Deep Work is not about habit at all, on its surface. It is about attention — his argument that the ability to focus without distraction on something cognitively demanding is both increasingly rare and increasingly valuable, and that most of what fills a workday is shallow busywork that feels productive and produces little. His formula stuck with me: high-quality work equals time spent multiplied by the intensity of your focus. Not hours. Hours times depth.

The thing nobody told me is that these are the same idea. Newport insists that deep focus is not a gift you either have or lack — it is a skill you train, and you train it exactly the way you would build any habit: with rituals, fixed times, a set place, and a stubborn refusal to reach for your phone the moment the work turns boring. His “rituals” are just Duhigg’s cues and routines wearing a lab coat. Deep work is a habit. And habits, in turn, are what make deep work possible: every trivial decision you have automated is a scrap of attention you didn’t spend, saved up for the one hour that matters.

So the chain, once I saw it, was hard to unsee. Habits automate the small things so your finite attention isn’t bled away on them. That freed attention is what deep work spends, all at once, on the work that is genuinely hard. Deep work is where the valuable output comes from. And the output, repeated, is what compounds into whatever we end up calling success. Habit is the foundation, focus is the peak, and neither does much without the other. A person with good habits and no depth stays busy and shallow; a person capable of depth but with no habits burns out chasing it on willpower alone.

What all three books share, underneath the productivity-shelf packaging, is a genuinely unglamorous psychology. You are not the person you intend to be; you are the person you repeat. Your attention is neither infinite nor free. And the way you get good at anything worth being good at is not a burst — it is a loop, run deliberately, protected fiercely, until the doing of it no longer requires a decision.

Which is a long way of saying the thing my to-do list was trying to tell me all along. The items that keep coming back aren’t proof that you’re failing. They’re just the ones that count. So you do them again. And again. And again.
entry.summary: What three books on habit and focus reveal about repetition, attention, and the quiet machinery behind success.

entry.title: Thinking right is the right thinking

link.title: Thinking right is the right thinking

entry.content:
For a while I went on a strange reading run — five books that, on paper, have almost nothing to do with each other. Two are about the wiring of the human mind. One is about how to change it. One is about sex and evolution. One is about why some societies ended up conquering others. I did not plan it as a course in anything. But somewhere in the middle I noticed they were all quietly telling the same uncomfortable story.

The story is this: the answer that feels obvious is usually the wrong one, and thinking well is mostly the work of distrusting it.

Kahneman’s Thinking, Fast and Slow and Dobelli’s The Art of Thinking Clearly make the case most directly. Both argue that the mind runs, by default, on fast and automatic shortcuts — and that those shortcuts fail in patterned, predictable ways. What struck me was that they say almost the same thing at different altitudes. Kahneman gives you the machinery: two systems, one impulsive and one effortful, with the lazy one usually winning. Dobelli skips the theory and hands you the field guide — ninety-nine named traps, from survivorship bias to the sunk-cost fallacy, each with a one-line warning. One explains why you err; the other helps you catch yourself doing it. You need both, which is why it never felt like reading the same book twice.

Adam Grant’s Think Again adds the move the other two leave implicit. Knowing your biases, it turns out, does almost nothing to stop them. The rarer skill is rethinking — holding your own conclusions loosely, treating beliefs as hypotheses rather than possessions, learning to feel curiosity instead of threat when you turn out to be wrong. Grant’s ideal is the scientist: someone who prefers doubt to certainty on purpose. Where Kahneman and Dobelli catalogue the errors, Grant says the errors will keep coming, and the only defence is a posture, not a checklist.

Then the run took a turn I didn’t expect. Matt Ridley’s The Red Queen is about evolution — why sex exists, how the endless arms race with parasites and rivals shaped us. But it reframed the whole bias conversation for me. Ridley’s point is that human nature is not designed; it is the residue of that race, each generation running flat out just to stay in place. And that means the mind’s “flaws” are not really flaws. A brain that jumps to patterns, fears loss more than it values gain, and trusts its own tribe was not malfunctioning on the savanna — it was surviving. This is where two versions of one fact both turn out to be true. Loss aversion is a bug in a spreadsheet and a feature on the plains; the mind is not broken, it is superbly built for a world that has stopped existing. Whether it counts as an error depends entirely on which environment you grade it against.

Jared Diamond’s Guns, Germs, and Steel is the same lesson at the scale of continents. The intuitive explanation for why some peoples dominated others is the flattering one — that they were cleverer, or harder, or simply better. Diamond spends the whole book dismantling exactly that story and replacing it with something duller and truer: geography. Wheat and cattle, an east–west axis, the accident of which animals could be domesticated and which diseases you had already survived. It is the most self-effacing kind of thinking there is — refusing the narrative that makes you the hero. And tellingly, his own critics accuse him of going too far, of letting geography erase human choices. They may be right too. Cause at one scale doesn’t cancel cause at another; the map and the decisions can both matter.

That is the thread. A psychologist, a writer, an organisational scientist, an evolutionary biologist, and a geographer, none of them talking to each other, all arriving in the same place. The comfortable answer flatters you. The true one usually costs you something — your certainty, your specialness, your story. “Thinking right” turned out not to be a set of correct opinions I could collect. It was closer to a stance: distrust the easy explanation, look underneath it for the structural one, and then, having found it, hold even that loosely enough to put back down.

Right thinking, it seems, is mostly the willingness to think again.
entry.summary: Five books from five different fields that quietly keep arriving at the same uncomfortable idea about how to think.

entry.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow — or, learning to distrust myself

link.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow — or, learning to distrust myself

entry.content:
I finished Daniel Kahneman’s Thinking, Fast and Slow with the strange sensation of having been let in on a secret about myself that I would rather not have known. That, I think, is the highest thing a book can do. It did not hand me facts so much as it quietly removed a floor I had been standing on — the belief that I am, more or less, a reasonable person who reaches conclusions by thinking.

The book’s famous framing is two systems: a fast, automatic mind that leaps to answers, and a slow, effortful one that is supposed to check them. But the real argument, the one that stays with you long after the individual examples fade, is darker and simpler. The fast mind runs almost everything, and the slow mind — the one we like to imagine is in charge — mostly arrives late to approve decisions that have already been made. We are not thinkers who occasionally feel. We are feelers who occasionally think, and then take the credit for it.

What lifts this above a clever academic point is Kahneman’s refusal to exempt himself. He spent a lifetime studying these errors, and he admits, without flinching, that he still commits them. He cannot see the mistakes coming any better than you can. There is something bracing about an expert telling you that expertise buys no immunity — that the biases he is describing are not other people’s failings but the standard operating conditions of every human mind, including the one reading this sentence.

The book does not make you smarter. It makes you suspicious of the feeling of being smart, which may be the more useful gift.

The lasting value of the book, for me, was never the catalogue of biases. You will forget most of their names within a month. It was a permanent, low-grade humility about my own certainty. After reading it, confidence stopped feeling like evidence. When something strikes me as obviously true, some small part of me now asks whether it seems true because it is — or merely because my fast mind has produced a smooth, coherent story and hidden the seams where the doubt should be. Kahneman’s phrase for this, what you see is all there is, has become a private alarm bell. The mind builds the most convincing case it can from whatever happens to be in front of it, and then forgets that anything was ever missing.

This is not a comfortable read, and it is not trying to be. It will not give you five tricks to think better by Friday. Its honesty is that it mostly cannot help you: knowing about a bias, it turns out, does almost nothing to stop you falling for it. What it can do is change the posture you take toward your own mind — from ownership to something closer to careful, slightly wary supervision.

If it leaves any practical residue, it is small and unglamorous. Slow down at the moments that actually matter. Distrust the answer that arrives too easily and too whole. Treat your own confidence as a symptom to examine rather than a verdict to trust. None of this is dramatic, and none of it is a cure. But over time it changes how you hold your opinions — more loosely, with a hand already prepared to let go.

I would not call it an enjoyable book. It is long, and it asks something of you on nearly every page. But it is one of the very few that genuinely altered the machinery I use to look at everything else, which is a rare and faintly unsettling thing to be able to say about a stack of paper. Years later, I still hear the alarm bell ring.
entry.summary: A review of Daniel Kahneman's book — less about what it says, more about what it quietly takes away from you.

entry.title: Turtle always wins
entry.content:
Slow, deliberate, unbothered. Photo by Wasted Time R, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.

When I was a child, I was told the story of the tortoise and the hare as if the lesson were obvious: slow and steady wins the race. I nodded along and forgot about it. It took me years to notice that the moral I had been handed was slightly wrong — or at least badly incomplete.

The hare did not lose because he was slow. He was, by any measure a stopwatch would recognise, faster. He lost because somewhere near the middle of the race he decided he had earned a rest. The tortoise won for one unglamorous reason: it never stopped walking.

I think about this more often than I would like to admit. We are all surrounded by people with real talent — quick minds, easy ability, the kind of early speed that makes everyone predict great things. And yet a surprising number of them simply drift. Not because they fail at something, but because they pause, and the pause quietly hardens into a habit, and the habit becomes a life.

Consistency is unfashionable. It photographs badly. Nobody posts about the ninth ordinary evening in a row spent on the same unfinished problem — the one with no breakthrough, no applause, no reason to keep going except that you said you would. But that ninth evening is where most of the real work of a life actually gets done. Intensity is a sprint you can admire from the stands; consistency is a walk you have to choose again every morning, usually on the mornings you least feel like it.

The tortoise’s secret was never speed. It was refusing to negotiate with the part of itself that wanted to sit down.

Dedication gets confused with passion, but they are not the same animal. Passion is loud and arrives for free; it shows up on the good days and disappears the instant the work turns dull. Dedication is quieter, and it costs something. It is what carries you across the flat, grey stretches — the plateaus where you are plainly working hard and plainly getting nowhere, and the only honest feedback is a calendar telling you that, yes, you showed up again.

Unhurried, and entirely certain of where it is going. Photo by Charles J. Sharp, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.

Those plateaus are where perseverance finally earns its name. Progress is almost never the smooth upward line we picture. It is a staircase with long, discouraging landings, and the people who reach the next step are simply the ones who kept their feet moving while standing on the flat part, trusting a rise they could not yet see.

I have come to believe that most things worth having are settled this way — not in one dramatic burst, but in the slow accumulation of unremarkable days. A language, a craft, a body of research, a friendship: none of them ever arrive. They are built, one small deposit at a time, by someone who kept turning up long after the excitement had worn off.

The hare, by the way, is still out there. He is gifted, he is quick, and he is asleep under a tree, certain he can make up the distance whenever he chooses. Perhaps he can. But the tortoise is not betting on his mistakes — it is just walking, at its own steady, stubborn pace, toward a finish line it fully intends to reach.

That is the whole trick, as far as I can tell. Not to be the fastest. Only to be the one who refuses to stop.
entry.summary: On consistency, dedication, and the quiet power of simply not stopping.
